speaker: interviewer
text: Arie Gedis. [PAUSES FOR 21 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Gedis, you were telling us that after you retired, you looked for something to do. And then you started to work as an extra in the movies. Tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I started. I like Hollywood. And I would like to know-- to know the-- the taste of Hollywood-- that all these pictures I used to see in my little town, you know. We had one movie theater, and I was working with my cousin. He was the-- the movie operator, you know-- make the picture. I used to help him, you know, roll over the-- the movies backwards, and forwards, and all that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I used to see every movie twice, three time sometimes. I knew the-- I knew the actors. I knew the-- the music writers. I knew the directors, you know, Cecil DeMille, and all the-- misc.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to have the taste of this Hollywood. I see all this when I was young kid, you know, with the big ape, you know, and all that. It's fantasy. I would like to see where they made it. I started as an extra, you know. I did a lot of work as an extra. And then ten years ago, you know, I-- there were a few parts. And I became a Screen Actors Guild member. I'm still today. I'm doing some commercials and things like that. I'm not going out of town too far, you know. I don't want to do that. But I'm doing, you know, every year a little bit. I've got a couple people, you know, I mean, that they call me often with-- it's all-- all SAG things, you know. And I belong to SAG and-- in touch with them. I get pretty good jobs with-- the first movie was was my first thing that I got.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember we made it in Malibu. And on the way back home, I was screaming like crazy. I said, me? SAG? I couldn't believe it, you know. So I found myself something to do in these years, you know, when you retire and you getting crazy what to do, how to do and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I paint a lot. Minnesota now, a big-- a big art dealer, you know, ordered from me some stuff. I sold some paintings a lot. I had some exhibitions here and also Wiesenthal Center, a few things. So I'm happy with myself. I'm doing something.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: The children are out of the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have grandchildren too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Julie. It's a girl. She is 14 years old-- a wonderful girl-- and Eric, 19. And he's-- he likes music too. But he likes rap music. It drives me crazy a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But he is talented. He's a good student. He's at UCLA. And I'm glad about it. And no more granddaughter-- one more granddaughter I have in Minnesota. She is three years old. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marissa.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] A good question-- don't tell.

speaker: interviewer
text: Marissa?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marissa. How did you know? I wanted you to say that. Marissa, she's a wonderful kid, you know. But the parents don't have a little too much, you know, patience. But she's wonderful. She is very smart for her age, you know, for three years old. She talks so nice, you know, and everything. And I'm glad about him. He's got a big family, my son. He's having to deal with it. He's doing all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they ordered some of things-- drawings and paintings over there, to-- for a big art seller, too. So I'm working with this too-- a little this, a little movies, a little children here, the grandchildren-- everything together. It gives me a little something in my life, you know, which I missed it for quite a while, for quite some years. So Mr. Gedis, let me ask you. Now that it's more than 50 years after the war, is there anything special you'd like to say for future generations-- any message you'd like to give to your children or grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one of the reasons I'm doing this tape now is not because I want to be on the tape, or I want to be in the movies, or I want to do some, you know, acting or something. That's not the point. The only thing I said to my wife, she didn't like it. She doesn't like to talk about it. When we had a movie, when we have on TV about the Holocaust, you know, she hardly can watch it, you know. She watches it, but she's taking it very hard, you know. I said to her, you know, for the future generations, for our children, let them see it, you know. When they'll see it, they will hear, we're talking-- telling our story. It's a different thing. It penetrates in your-- in your brain different, you know. Then you hear about it! You watch a movie, you see somebody get killed, and you drinking a coffee. You enjoy it. I mean, you don't care. He got killed, he got killed. It's a movie. But when you feel the real taste of it, you know, in life, the way it looks, you know, when hundreds and thousands of people, millions of people are getting killed for nothing, you know, for being-- just being a Jew. That's what I want the generations to know about it. And that's why we did this thing here. And I suppose that most of the people that doing it are doing it for this thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Thank you very much, Mr. Gedis. We really appreciate your taking the time to give us your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: I appreciate it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now we'd like to meet your wife first, and then take a look at your photographs, and some of your artwork.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Gedis, why don't you introduce us to your lovely wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my wife, Eva Gedis.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Mrs. Gedis, is there anything you'd like to say to your husband at the end of this tape-- anything you would like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had a wonderful life-- nice family. And I want to thank him to push me to make the tape. [LAUGHS] But I didn't even want to do it. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought she will kill me, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I'm glad she didn't. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: It seems you're still here. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. We really appreciate your taking the time for us today and allowing us--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a great pleasure to do it for our future generations.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank-- thank you, for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hopefully, they'll know about it, what's going on, you know-- my children, and your children the same, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. Tell us about this picture, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: The top-- the left side-- the top left side, that's that my father, Samuel, and my mother, Rachel.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken-- I can't tell you exactly when it is. But it was taken many years, you know. I found this picture from my-- with my uncle, you know. I don't really remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where it was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland-- Poland. Yeah. On the right side-- on the right side here is my mother, Rachel, my father, Samuel, and my grandfather, Joseph-- Josef.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this your mother's father or your father's father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's father.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken approximately the same time as the other one was taken. I can't remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Ostrog-- Ostrog. And that's another town not far from Zdolbunow, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: --about 30 miles, yes, in Poland. That picture to the right is the family, 1929, when my uncle came from United States to visit us in the city of Ostrog. In the front, on the right side, is my Uncle Aaron and his wife. My father and mother are next to them, my Uncle Louie next to them, and then my grandpa and grandma sitting in front, the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the head of the table.

speaker: interviewee
text: The head of the table. The other side are my cousins, you know. It's my auntie's children. My Uncle Pearl and Isaac and their children are sitting the other side. That's a-- that's a Passover. It was a Passover, 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get this picture after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got it from my uncle when I-- when we came to United States-- from my Uncle Louie. He was in Boston. We got it from him. He had a few pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: How come you weren't in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a kid. I remember I was sick in that time, you know, from traveling something. I didn't feel good. So they put me in bed, you know. Yeah. Bed, I remember good. This is a picture of the youth organization called Gordonia-- the name of our own, David Gordon, which was a big pioneer for the Israelis to build kibbutzim and to build a future for Israel. Gordonia, we called it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Zdolbuniv. It was taken in Zdolbuniv.

speaker: interviewer
text: Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately-- what-- '29, '30, I would say.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where are you in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm on the top-- the middle-- the top in the middle somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the back row?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the back row.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the center?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm. Well, that's my picture when I was young fellow, approximately the same time with the other picture the group it was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately 1930?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1930-- something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the picture taken again?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Zdolbuniv-- the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell us your name again, please, for the record.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the name was, in those days, was Arie Gekdishman. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, on the right side is me and my two friends. It's in Salzburg, Austria, before we passed the Alps to Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in-- in 1944-- no-- 9--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, something like that, I think, yeah. Do you know the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of the group, the kibbutz, we called, in [NON-ENGLISH] in Italy-- before we left Italy. Now on the top row, the second on the left, is my wife, Eva.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Top row--

speaker: interviewee
text: Top row.

speaker: interviewer
text: --fourth from the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fourth from the left is me, Arie Gedis. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: The second.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the second of the left is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Second.

speaker: interviewee
text: --is my wife, Eva.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's in the middle row.

speaker: interviewer
text: Middle.

speaker: interviewee
text: Middle. Well, they'll find her anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] And what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1947. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The picture on the right is people come from all over to Cyprus-- come with the little packages-- not much carrying with them-- to start a new life.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive in Cyprus?

speaker: interviewer
text: And the picture on the top left-- oh, excuse me-- on the bottom-- on the bottom?

speaker: interviewee
text: The bottom, I'm working a little art for an album from Cyprus.

speaker: interviewer
text: No-- on the bottom right, the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Bottom--

speaker: interviewer
text: --the still people arriving?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's one picture, I think-- people arriving, yeah, from all over to Cyprus.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now the bottom left-- tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: The bottom left is me working on an album from Cyprus. We had a few people. One was the editor when I was the artist. We put it together, you know, for the Keren Kayemeth-- for Israel-- Museum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what's the picture on the top left?

speaker: interviewee
text: The top left is early from-- made from copper-- about the camps-- especially the camp of Cyprus, the people that came from all over, which is taken away from us by a rabbi to a-- to the Keren Kayemeth Exhibition.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's standing with that picture? Is that you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I am standing over there. That's me, Arie Gedis.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Arie Gedis, standing there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the last picture. It was taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken again?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the Keren Kayemeth Israel Museum-- Art Museum-- in Jerusalem. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Well, that's a picture of me, Arie Gedis, my wife, Eva, and our daughter, Rachel, in 1931--

speaker: interviewee
text: '51 in Israel. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Now this is a commendation that I got from the Israeli government for participating in the-- the-- it's called the [NON-ENGLISH], for participating in the war, Kadesh, in 1956. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: On the upper row, this is Steven Gedis, my son. On the left side?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left side. Next to him is myself, Arie Gedis, and then my granddaughter, Julie. And then my son-in-law, Victor Kosasky, and then Eric, my grandson. The lower from the left is Heidi. And then next to her is her little daughter, Marissa, and my wife, Eva. Next to her is Rachel, my daughter. And that's about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm glad you remembered.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: She's making already partially a model.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is a picture of the shtetl, made by myself, Arie Gedis, and the memories, the old memories of the [NON-ENGLISH]-- of a little girl with a grandmother going to get some things to buy; and the Jew standing in the side, looking-- what will be tomorrow? Those are the memories that come by, and all. You think about them many years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What medium did you use to make this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Use waterproof ink. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] It's called Although I wasn't in a concentration camp, but I heard so many things from my wife, from people that were over there, the stories about them. And I decided to create something that reminds people what really happened-- the faces, the expressions, the dying. That's what-- that's why I made this picture.
